coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-driven nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots decorated g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme composite with enhanced photocatalytic activity and mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Jiankui
Zhang, Xiaorui
Jiang, Caiyun
Huang, Wenxin
Wang, Yuping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme composites were synthesized by in-situ calcination and hydrothermal method and the novel g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs was constructed by introducing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQs) on the interface of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 catalyst. UV–vis spectroscopy data showed that the improved visible light utilization efficiency of the catalyst is attributed to the composition of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme heterojunction and the addition of NCQs. Photocurrent response analysis and fluorescence intensity measurements showed that the high separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs composite. In addition, the excellent structural stability of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs can be proved by the analysis data of cycle experiments and XRD diffraction peaks of catalysts before and after experiments. Obviously, the above experimental data and analysis demonstrated the outstanding photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs. Free radical capture experimental data analysis confirmed that superoxide ion radicals (·O2 −) and holes (h+) play a major role in the reaction process, and the possible photocatalytic mechanism of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs was proposed to further understand its enhanced photocatalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4/Bi2WO6
Z-scheme heterojunction
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Photocatalytic mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of single NCQs, g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6
The transient photocurrent responses and photoluminescence (PL) spectra

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The adsorption-desorption equilibrium of RhB over the synthesized catalysts in dark.
XRD patterns of g-C3N4, Bi2WO6, NCQs, g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 with different mass ratios of Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs with different mass ratios of NCQs.
FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4, Bi2WO6, NCQs, g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 with different mass ratios of Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs with different mass ratios of NCQs.
TEM images and HRTEM images of g-C3N4 (a), NCQs (b), Bi2WO6 (c), g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 (d), and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs (e, f, g).
(a) XPS survey spectrum of g-C3N4, Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs; and the corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s; (d) O 1s; (e) Bi 4f and (f)W 4f.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm (a) and corresponding pore size distribution curve (b) of g-C3N4, Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs.
UV–vis DRS spectra of the as-prepared samples of g-C3N4, Bi2WO6, gb-0.02 and gbn-2.
The photocurrent response curves of g-C3N4, Bi2WO6, gb-0.02 and gbn-2.
The photocatalytic degradation, the pseudo-first-order kinetics fitted curves and the photodegradation rate constants of RhB (a, c, e) and TC (b, d, f) based on the prepared samples, respectively.

(a) the cycle experiment of RhB solution over gbn-2 catalyst under visible-light irradiation, (b) XRD patterns of the fresh and the used gbn-2, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB at different time intervals with gbn-2.
The photocatalytic degradation curves of RhB over gbn-2 with various trapping scavengers.
Schematic diagram of the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs under visible-light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process for g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs composites.
Specific surface area analysis of the as-prepared samples.
Calculated value of the CB and VB potentials of g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-driven nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots decorated g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme composite with enhanced photocatalytic activity and mechanism insight

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Material Science, Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Cycling and Pollution Control, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing, 210046, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science
Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Cycling and Pollution Control

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme composites were synthesized by in-situ calcination and hydrothermal method and the novel g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs was constructed by introducing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQs) on the interface of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 catalyst. UV–vis spectroscopy data showed that the improved visible light utilization efficiency of the catalyst is attributed to the composition of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme heterojunction and the addition of NCQs. Photocurrent response analysis and fluorescence intensity measurements showed that the high separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs composite. In addition, the excellent structural stability of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs can be proved by the analysis data of cycle experiments and XRD diffraction peaks of catalysts before and after experiments. Obviously, the above experimental data and analysis demonstrated the outstanding photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs. Free radical capture experimental data analysis confirmed that superoxide ion radicals (·O2 −) and holes (h+) play a major role in the reaction process, and the possible photocatalytic mechanism of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs was proposed to further understand its enhanced photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the photocatalytic technique has been extensively investigated and rapidly developed in the field of environmental remediation because it can utilize green and sustainable solar energy [1]. So far, various single-component catalysts have been widely studied, such as TiO2 [2], ZnO [3], WO3 [4], CdS [5], Ag2S [6], and so on. However, the theoretical research and practical application of these catalyst materials are hindered due to the low separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, low utilization of visible light, and poor stability [7–9]. Hence, in order to further promote the catalytic performance of single-component catalysts, many measures including metal/non-metal loading [10,11], microstructure control [12] and heterostructure construction [13] have been adopted in theoretical research to solve the above problems. Among the above ways, the construction of heterojunctions is an efficient and feasible way to promote the photocatalytic activity of catalysts [13], especially for Z-scheme heterojunctions.

Recently, Z-scheme heterojunctions have attracted extensive attention and investigation because they exhibit the advantages of extended visible light absorption range, improved charge transfer efficiency, and enhanced redox capacity [14,15]. In particular, the research of g-C3N4-based Z-scheme heterojunction has made rapid progress and development due to its simple preparation method, stable physical and chemical properties, and excellent photoelectric properties [16–18]. In addition, among many g-C3N4-based Z-scheme heterojunction catalysts, g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme heterojunction exhibits outstanding photocatalytic performance due to its suitable band gap matching [19,20]. This band gap structure can maintain a strong redox capacity by recombining weakly reducing electrons and weakly oxidizing holes and promote efficient separation of photo-generated carriers [9]. However, despite the great progress and development have been made in Z-scheme g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 photocatalytic heterojunction, there are still some problems and challenges: (i) The catalytic performance of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 is suppressed due to the insufficient utilization of visible light [21]. (ii) The redox capacity of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 will inevitably decrease with the separation and transfer process of electron-hole pairs, which is detrimental to the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency [22]. (iii) Due to the insufficient active sites, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 is also suppressed [1].

Besides, it is reported that when the electron transfer mediators is distributed in the Z-scheme heterojunction catalyst, the catalytic performance of the catalyst is improved [1,23]. NCQs, as a novel zero-dimensional carbon material with a size below 10 nm, has been widely used in sensors, photovoltaic devices and catalysis because of its unique optical and electronic properties [8]. More importantly, when NCQs are applied in the field of photocatalysis, it has the following advantages: (i) The light absorption capacity of the catalyst can be enhanced due to the up-conversion photoluminescence properties of NCQs [8]. (ii) The charge transport of heterojunction catalysts can be promoted because of the small size and excellent electronic conductivity of NCQs [23]. (iii) Since NCQs can be used as electron acceptors and donors, the electrons in the reaction system can be more easily transferred to the interface of the heterojunction catalyst [24]. Hence, if NCQs is used as an electronic mediator in heterojunction materials, it is highly possible to construct a novel g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs Z-scheme heterojunction with superior photocatalytic activity.

In this work, a novel NCQs modified g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme catalyst was constructed and synthesized successfully, in which NCQs served as excellent light absorber and electronic transmission medium. With rhodamine B (RhB) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) as simulated pollutants, the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized catalysts was evaluated under visible light irradiation. Compared with pure g-C3N4, Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6, g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs showed the largest photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the physicochemical properties and experimental verification of the as-prepared catalysts were studied systematically, and the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs was also proposed and discussed.

Melamine (C3H6N6), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), absolute ethyl alcohol, sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), Citric acid (C6H8O7) and urea were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. The experimental reagents employed in this work were of analytical grade and had not been further purified during use. The experimental water was pure water in all synthetic materials and experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of single NCQs, g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6
ce:section.ce:para: The g-C3N4 nanosheets and NCQs aqueous solution were synthesized using the preparation method of our previously reported article [23]. Bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6) was prepared by a combination of traditional hydrothermal and calcination methods reported in the literature with minor modifications [25]. Typically, Na2WO4·2H2O and Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (1:2 M ratio) were respectively dissolved in 20 ml of aqueous solution and sonicated for 30 min to make them uniformly dispersed, and the two solutions mentioned above were mixed well and stirred for 2 h. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 170 °C for 20 h. Finally, the pure Bi2WO6 was obtained by centrifugation, washing with deionized water, drying at 80 °C, and then calcining at 300 °C in air for 1 h.

The g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs composite was fabricated by in-situ calcination and physical deposition. Initially, the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 was synthesized by an in-situ calcination method. Briefly, the prepared g-C3N4 (0.1 g) and a certain amount of Bi2WO6 were dissolved in a beaker containing 50 mL of water and ultrasonicized for 30 min, and then the obtained suspension was further magnetically stirred for 1 h. After that, the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 catalysts were obtained by centrifuging the above suspension to remove water, drying at 90 °C and then calcining at 300 °C for 1 h in a muffle furnace. The obtained catalysts were expressed as gb-0.005, gb-0.02, gb-0.04 and gb-0.06 for the Bi2WO6 feeding amount of 0.005, 0.02, 0.04 and 0.06 g, respectively. Subsequently, a certain amount of NCQs was added to a suspension containing 0.1 g of synthesized g-C3N4/Bi2WO6, and then magnetically stirred for 12 h to evenly disperse NCQs on the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 interface. In this way, g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs containing different amounts (1, 2 and 4 mg) of NCQs were successfully prepared and denoted as gbn-1, gbn-2 and gbn-4, respectively. The preparation process of the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs heterojunction catalyst is shown in Scheme 1 .

The crystal phases of the synthesized catalysts were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max 2500VL/PC). The transmission electron microscope (HRTEM ZEOL JEM-2100) was employed to observe the morphology and lattice structure of the samples. The infrared absorption spectra of the catalysts were recorded by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, HYPERION 2000). The surface elemental composition and chemical states of the materials were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Escalab 250). Specific surface areas of the catalysts were measured by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2050). UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary 5000) was employed to examine the absorption wavelength of the samples and calculate the band gap of the samples through this data analysis. The fluorescence intensity of the catalysts were identified by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA FM-4P-TCSPC). The photocurrent of the samples were carried out by an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E).

RhB and TC were used as simulated pollutants to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the above catalysts under visible light radiation. The visible light source was provided by an 800W Xe lamp with a cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm). In a typical experiment, 20 mg of catalyst and 50 mL of 10 mg/L RhB (or TC) aqueous solution are mixed well and stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium (Fig. 1 ). At irradiation time intervals of 10 min, the mixed solution (3 mL) was sampled and centrifuged. Then the absorption wavelength of RhB (or TC) supernatant was analyzed at 554 nm (or 360 nm) to determine their remaining concentration by an UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The crystalline structures of the above as-prepared samples are investigated by X-ray diffraction and shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a shows the XRD patterns of pure g-C3N4, Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 with different mass ratios of Bi2WO6. The diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 are in good agreement with the standard XRD pattern of graphitic carbon nitride (JCPDS 87–1526), and the peaks at 13.1° and 27.4° can be detected as the (100) and (002) crystal planes of pure g-C3N4, respectively [26]. For Bi2WO6, all the detected XRD patterns are consistent with the orthorhombic phase of Bi2WO6 (JCPDS 73–1126) [27]. Moreover, it can be observed that the diffraction peaks of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 with different mass ratios of Bi2WO6 are similar to that of Bi2WO6, and the diffraction peak weakens at 27.4° with the increase of Bi2WO6 content, indicating the successful preparation of the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 composite. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2b, the pure NCQs shows a broad peak ascribed to the disordered carbon atoms [8]. Compared with the diffraction peaks of gb-0.02, no obvious changes can be observed in the XRD patterns of gbn-1, gbn-2 and gbn-4 after the introduction of NCQs at the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 interface, indicating that NCQs cannot affect the structure of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6. At the same time, the characteristic diffraction peaks of NCQs are not shown on the XRD patterns of gbn-1, gbn-2 and gbn-4, which may be due to the small amount and high dispersion of NCQs [23]. However, it can be observed that the following TEM and XPS analysis can prove that many NCQs are uniformly distributed at the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 heterojunction interface. Therefore, the above analysis results show that the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs catalyst was successfully constructed.

Fig. 3 exhibits the changes in functional bonds during the synthesis of the prepared catalyst. In the FT-IR spectrum of Bi2WO6 (Fig. 3a), the sharp peaks at 572 cm−1 and 731 cm−1 are assigned to the Bi–O, W–O stretching and W–O–W bridging stretching, respectively [28]. Pure g-C3N4 displays characteristic peaks at 1237–1652 cm−1 and 809 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching mode of C–N heterocycles and the triazine vibrations, respectively [29]. In addition, it can be observed that the main characteristic absorption peaks of g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 exist on the infrared spectrum of the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs complexes, further indicating that the structure of the catalysts is stable and unchanged. However, as shown in Fig. 3b, there was no absorption peak of NCQs on the spectral curve of the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs heterojunction, which is attributed to a small amount of NCQs and is consistent with the XRD analysis results.

The TEM images of the as-prepared samples are characterized and revealed in Fig. 4 . As displayed in Fig. 4a, the stacked nanoflake structure can be observed on the TEM image of pure g-C3N4, which can provide more active sites to promote photocatalytic reactions [8]. It can be seen from Fig. 4b that NCQs are highly uniformly dispersed and the size of the particle size is about 2.5 nm [23]. The irregular two-dimensional nanoplates are observed on the TEM image of pure Bi2WO6 (Fig. 4c), which coincides with the reported literature [30]. In Fig. 4d, it can be concluded that the nanoplate of Bi2WO6 was tightly loaded on the interface of flake-shaped g-C3N4, which is beneficial to the improvement of the electronic structure between the g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 interface and promotes effective charge carrier transfer. In addition, some uniformly dispersed black dots can be observed on the HRTEM images of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6NCQs (Fig. 4e, f and 4g), indicating that NCQs successfully modified the interface of g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6. Meanwhile, NCQs can also act as active site and collector to improve the effective separation of charge carriers and enhance photocatalytic activity [31].

The chemical composition and elemental state of the above materials are further studied by XPS and are listed in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5a, the full spectrum elements of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs are detected, which are consistent with the chemical composition of the above materials and XRD analysis, respectively. From the XPS spectrum of C 1s in Fig. 5b, the peaks at 287.9 and 284.6 eV can be attributed to the N–CN coordination and the C–C bonds of g-C3N4, respectively [32]. Meanwhile, compared with g-C3N4, the C1s spectrum of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs shifts slightly (by 0.2 eV) to high binding energies, which may be attributed to the charge transfer at the g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 interfaces [23]. The peak intensity at 284.6 eV of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs is higher than that of g-C3N4, which can be considered to be caused by the introduction of NCQs [24]. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 5c), the binding energy peaks at 398.2 eV of g-C3N4 and 398.5 eV of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs can be assigned to the CN–C in g-C3N4 [33], Such a shift (by 0.2 eV) of N1s may be caused by the close interaction between g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 [9]. The other two peaks at 399.3 and 400.6 eV correspond to the N-(C)3 and the surface NHx groups, respectively [34]. The O 1s spectrum of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs (Fig. 5d) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 529.9 and 531.2eV, which can be ascribed to the status of W–O and Bi–O, respectively [35]. In Fig. 5e, the peaks located at 158.5 and 163.8 eV can be assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, indicating that the oxidation state of Bi3+ exists in g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs [36]. the W 4f spectrum (Fig. 5f) shows two peaks at 34.6 and 36.8 eV, which derives from the W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2 state in g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs, respectively [37]. Therefore, the XPS results further illustrate that the construction of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs heterojunctions can be perfectly achieved.

The specific surface area is always regarded as an important factor in the study of catalyst performance [38]. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the prepared g-C3N4, Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs are measured and IV-type isotherms are displayed, indicating the mesoporous structure of the above materials. In addition, the pore size distribution corresponding to the above materials is shown in Fig. 6b, which further confirms that the above catalyst conforms to the characteristics of the mesoporous structure (2–50 nm). The specific surface area and pore structure of the above materials are calculated and summarized in Table 1 . It can be known that the specific surface area of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs (13.6 m2 g−1) is 3.02 times larger than that of Bi2WO6 (4.5 m2 g−1), which indicates that g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs can provide more active sites to promote its photocatalytic activity. However, the specific surface area of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs is slightly lower than that of g-C3N4, which may be attributed to the assembly and stacking of the flakelike structures of g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 [39]. Therefore, it can be concluded that a large specific surface area can promote the catalytic activity of the catalyst, which is consistent with the following experimental results of photocatalytic activity.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was employed to investigate the characteristics of light absorption and energy band of the catalysts and the results are depicted in Fig. 7 . It can be seen that the edges of the absorption peaks of g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 are detected at approximately 470 and 460 nm, corresponding to their band gap energies of 2.64 and 2.7, respectively [16,40]. Compared with g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6, both of gb-0.02 and gbn-2 show enhanced light absorption capabilities, which should be attributed to the Z-scheme heterostructure of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 and the up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics of NCQs [41]. In addition, the conduction band (CB) and valance band (VB) edge potentials of the g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 were calculated by the Mulliken electronegativity theory [42], and the calculation results are displayed in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The transient photocurrent responses and photoluminescence (PL) spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of a catalyst is usually closely related to the separation efficiency of its photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The efficient separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs means excellent photocatalytic performance [43]. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the periodic on/off photocurrent responses of g-C3N4, Bi2WO6, gb-0.02 and gbn-2 were measured under the irradiation of visible light. Compared with g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6, gb-0.02 shows a larger photocurrent, indicating that the structure of the Z-scheme heterojunction facilitates the transfer and separation of photogenerated charges. Moreover, the photocurrent of gbn-2 is the largest among the above catalysts, which further proves that NCQs can be used as carriers and collectors of electron-hole pairs to promote the effective separation of photogenerated charges. In addition, in order to further understand the outstanding catalytic performance of gbn-2, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum (Fig. 8b) of the above catalysts were measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Generally speaking, a catalyst exhibits a weak PL fluorescence intensity, which means that the catalyst has a strong charge separation efficiency [44]. It can be observed in Fig. 8b that pure g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 show the emission peaks at about 450 nm and 430 nm, respectively, which is consistent with the reported literature [17]. Moreover, the order of PL fluorescence intensity of the samples is: Bi2WO6 > g-C3N4 > gb-0.02 > gbn-2, which further shows that the photogenerated charge separation efficiency can be enhanced by the design of Z-scheme heterojunction and the addition of NCQs.

As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, RhB and TC are employed as simulated pollutants to evaluate the catalytic performance of the catalyst under visible light radiation. In Fig. 9a, it can be found that pure g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 show low photocatalytic activity under the same experimental conditions, and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency is 8% and 52% in 45 min, respectively. Moreover, g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 composites exhibit higher photocatalytic activity than pure g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6, which can be attributed to the construction of a Z-scheme heterojunction between g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 composites first increases and then decreases with the increase of Bi2WO6 content, which indicates that the introduction of excessive Bi2WO6 may cover the active sites of g-C3N4 and hinder the effective migration of charge carriers, resulting in reduced photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6. Therefore, there is an optimal ratio of Bi2WO6 loaded on the surface of g-C3N4. As can be seen from Fig. 9a, the photocatalytic degradation rate of gb-0.02 is the largest one of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 composites, which means that gb-0.02 is the best composite ratio of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 binary composites. When NCQs are introduced at the interface of the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 composite, the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs ternary heterojunction exhibits significantly improved photocatalytic performance, which proves that the introduction of NCQs can enhance the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and the absorption of visible light, leading to excellent photocatalytic activity of the catalysts. In addition, it can be seen from our previously reported literature (Fig. S1) that compared with the corresponding g-C3N4, WO3 and g-C3N4/WO3, g-C3N4/NQDs, WO3/NQDs and g-C3N4/WO3/NQDs show higher photocatalytic activity, which indicates that NQDs plays an important synergistic role in the photocatalytic reaction system, thereby improving their photocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB and TC [23]. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs will decrease with the addition of excessive NCQs, which can be described as the excessive NCQs supported on the catalysts surface will hinder the absorption of visible light of the catalysts, leading to the above phenomenon. Therefore, there is an optimal amount of NCQs deposited on the surface of the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6. It can be known from Fig. 9a, compared with the degradation of RhB by other g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs composites, gbn-2 exhibits the largest photocatalytic activity, indicating that the NCQs content in the gbn-2 composite is the most suitable. In addition, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model is used to study the photocatalytic degradation process [45]. The kinetic curves and reaction rate constants of the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over all the catalysts are shown in Fig. 9c and e, respectively. It can be concluded that the above reaction process is in line with the pseudo-first-order kinetic, and gbn-2 shows the largest rate constant (0.641 min−1) among all the materials, which is about 45.7, 3.9 and 2.0 times larger than that of the Bi2WO6 (0.014 min−1), g-C3N4 (0.163 min−1) and gb-0.02 (0.321 min−1), respectively.

In order to further illustrate the excellent photocatalytic activity of the synthesized g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs, TC was also used as the target pollutant to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the above samples. As shown in Fig. 9b, after 60 min of visible light radiation, gbn-2 shows the largest photocatalytic degradation efficiency in the above materials. The removal order of TC is gbn-2 > gb-0.02 > g-C3N4 > Bi2WO6, which is consistent with the photocatalytic degradation rate of RhB. Moreover, it can be concluded from Fig. 9d that the degradation reaction of TC can be quantitatively fitted by the pseudo-first-order kinetic, and gbn-2 shows the fastest degradation rate compared with g-C3N4, Bi2WO6 and gb-0.02. Meanwhile, the rate constants of the above catalysts are depicted in Fig. 9f, the k value of gbn-2 (0.243 min−1) is about 4.3, 1.8 and 1.3 times of Bi2WO6 (0.056 min−1), g-C3N4 (0.130 min−1) and gb-0.02 (0.177 min−1), respectively. The above experimental analysis shows that the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6NCQs heterojunction is universal for degrading pollutants such as dyes and antibiotics, and shows excellent catalytic performance.

The stability and reusability are particularly important in the theoretical research and practical application of the photocatalysts. Fig. 10 a displays the cyclic experiment of photocatalytic degradation of RhB by gbn-2. Compared with the first photocatalytic degradation efficiency of gbn-2, the photocatalytic activity of gbn-2 did not decrease significantly after five cycles of degradation of RhB, indicating that gbn-2 exhibits excellent stability under visible light radiation. In addition, the crystal structure of gbn-2 before and after the experiment was analyzed by XRD and shown in Fig. 10b. Compared with the XRD of the original gbn-2, the crystal structure of the gbn-2 did not change significantly after five cycles of degradation experiments, and no other peaks appeared, which further illustrates the outstanding photocatalytic stability and reusability of gbn-2. Moreover, In order to further determine the catalytic performance of the catalyst gbn-2, as shown in Fig. 10c, the degree of degradation of RhB was detected using UV scanning technology. From the UV scan spectrum of the photocatalytic degradation product, it can be known that after 45 min of light irradiation, the characteristic absorption peak of RhB is significantly reduced, indicating that the target pollutant can be completely photocatalytically degraded.

In order to further understand the reaction mechanism and main active species in photocatalytic degradation, the trapping experiments were performed in the presence of different scavengers, where benzoquinone (BQ), disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (Na2-EDTA) and isopropanol (IPA) were selected as the superoxide ion radicals (·O2 −), holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) scavengers, respectively [46]. As displayed in Fig. 11 , when the scavengers BQ and Na2-EDTA are introduced into the photocatalytic degradation reaction system, the removal efficiency of RhB is significantly reduced, which shows that O2 − and h+ play a key role in the reaction process [47]. In addition, the addition of IPA has a slight inhibitory effect on the degradation efficiency of RhB, which proves that the active species ·OH plays an auxiliary role in the reaction system.

Based on the above experimental analysis, the traditional double charge transfer theory is proposed and displayed in Fig. 12 a. Under visible light radiation, g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 are excited to generate electron-hole pairs. Holes (h+) are distributed in their valence band (VB), and the excited photo-generated electrons (e−) are transferred to their corresponding conduction bands (CB) [48]. Subsequently, due to the proper band gap energy positions of g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6, the photo-generated electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 are transferred to the CB of Bi2WO6 and the holes in the VB of Bi2WO6 are also transferred to the VB of g-C3N4. Meanwhile, owing to the excellent reservoir properties of NCQs, the photo-generated electrons are more easily transferred to the catalyst interface reaction, which promotes the effective transfer and separation of photo-generated charges [24]. However, the dissolved O2 in the reaction system cannot be reduced by the e− in the CB of Bi2WO6, because its CB potential of Bi2WO6 is more positive than the redox potential of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE) [49]. Similarly, the OH− in the reaction system cannot be oxidized by the h+ in the VB of g-C3N4 because of the more positive standard redox potential ·OH/OH− (2.40 eV vs. NHE) [50] than the VB of g-C3N4. So, the above experimental results indicate that the charge carrier transfer path of gbn-2 is not suitable for the traditional double charge transfer theory.

Based on the above discussion, a possible Z-scheme photocatalytic reaction mechanism is proposed and can well explain the degradation process of RhB. As illustrated in Fig. 12b, NCQs can be used as a bridge for charge transfer between g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6 due to its small size and outstanding electronic conductivity, which promotes the recombination of the e− in the CB of Bi2WO6 and the h+ in the VB of g-C3N4. The above phenomenon also promotes the effective separation and accumulation of the e− of g-C3N4 and the h+ of Bi2WO6, further improving the photocatalytic performance of gbn-2. Moreover, NCQs can be donors and acceptors of electrons, making it easier for electrons to migrate to the surfaces of g-C3N4, Bi2WO6 and NCQs, which also results in effective separation of electron-hole pairs [51]. Subsequently, the e− in the CB of g-C3N4 is captured by dissolved O2 in the reaction system to produce ·O2 −, because the electron potential of g-C3N4 is smaller than the standard redox potential of O2/·O2 −. Meanwhile, RhB or TC will be directly oxidized and degraded into small molecules by the generated ·O2 − and h+ in the VB of Bi2WO6 due to their strong oxidation ability [52]. In addition, the potential of the h+ in the VB of Bi2WO6 is higher than the standard redox potential of ·OH/OH−, so a part of the holes oxidizes H2O to form ·OH, which makes it participate in the reaction system. The above analysis results show that the three active species of e−, h+ and ·OH are involved in the photocatalytic reaction, and are consistent with the results of the capture experiment. The reaction equations (1)–(4) that may be involved in the photocatalytic reaction process are shown below.
(1)
g -
C 3
N 4
/ B
i 2
W
O 6
/ N C D s + h v →
e −
+
h +
(2)
O 2
+
e −
→ ⋅
O 2
−
(3)
H 2
O +
h +
→ ⋅ O
H −
(4)
⋅
O 2
−
/
h +
/ ⋅ O H + R h B / T C →
H 2
O + C
O 2

In summary, the novel g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs Z-scheme catalyst was successfully constructed by a combination of in-situ calcination and hydrothermal method. In g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs heterojunction materials, NCQs act as acceptors and donors of electrons and as bridges of charge transfer, which together promote the effective transfer and separation of charge carriers at the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs interface. Therefore, compared to g-C3N4, Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 composite, the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs shows the largest degradation rate for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB and TC. UV–vis DRS analysis showed that the improved photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs also benefited from the construction of a Z-scheme heterojunction between g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6, which extended the absorption range and utilization of visible light of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/NCQs. Therefore, this work provides innovative feasibility and new ideas to improve the catalytic performance and explore the reaction mechanism of Z-scheme heterojunction materials by introducing NCQs.
Jiankui Jia: Conceptualization, Methodology. Xiaorui Zhang: Methodology. Caiyun Jiang: Supervision. Wenxin Huang: Validation, Data curation. Yuping Wang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 51578295), the National Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161479), and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Chemical Pollution Control and Resources Reuse (Nanjing University of Science and Technology), Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province. Additionally, we also appreciate Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials and a project funded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.
